title: Conditional Expressions
description: A very Kotlin-specific trick for writing cleaner if statements.

slides.title: The Old Way 🧱
slides.content:
## Setting Variables

Often, we use `if/else` just to figure out what value to assign to a single variable.

In older languages like Java, this required creating the variable first, then using an `if/else` block with curly braces to set it.

slides.title: The Verbose Way 🐢
slides.content: Look how long this is just to figure out a text string:

slides.title: Expressions in Kotlin ✨
slides.content:
## If as an Expression

In Kotlin, `if` is an **expression**, meaning it actually *returns a value*. 

We can assign the result of an `if/else` block directly to a variable right when we create it!

slides.title: The Kotlin Way 🚀
slides.content:
Notice how we declare `val result` and immediately assign it to the `if` block. 

No curly braces needed for single statements! It's clean and concise.
slides.codeSnippet:
val score = 85

// Look how clean this is!
val result = if (score >= 50) "Pass" else "Fail"

println(result)

quizData.question: Why is assigning an `if/else` as an expression directly to a variable better?

options.text: It requires fewer curly braces and looks cleaner.
It is the only way to write an if statement in Kotlin.

quizData.explanation: Using `if` as an expression reduces boilerplate code lines and lets you use the safer `val` instead of `var`.
